title: PN 227/26: BUDGET 2026 - INCOME TAX AND NATIONAL INSURANCE PROPOSALS various taxation and National Insurance measures.
text:
INCOME TAX DIVISION
The Treasury
Government Office, Douglas
Isle of Man, British Isles
Yn Tashtey IM1 3TX
Telephone: (01624) 685400
Assessor of Income Tax E mail: incometax@itd.treasury.gov.im
Website: www.gov.im/incometax
PRACTICE NOTE
PN 227/26 Date: 17 February 2026
BUDGET 2026 - INCOME TAX AND NATIONAL INSURANCE PROPOSALS
In his Budget speech today, the Minister for the Treasury, the Hon. Chris Thomas, MHK, announced
various taxation and National Insurance measures.
This Practice Note contains further information regarding the changes.
1. Income Tax Allowances and Rates of Tax .................................................................................. 1
2. TT Homestay .......................................................................................................................... 2
3. Nursing Expenses .................................................................................................................... 2
4. National Insurance Contributions .............................................................................................. 3
5. Increase in the State Pension Age ............................................................................................. 3
1. INCOME TAX ALLOWANCES AND RATES OF TAX
The following rates and allowances will apply for the 2026/2027 tax year commencing on 6 April 2026:-
Personal allowances 2026/2027 2025/2026
£ £
Single person 17,0001 14,7501
Married couple/civil partners (combined) 34,0001 29,5001
Additional Personal Allowance 6,400 6,400
Blind person 2,900 2,900
Disabled person 2,900 2,900
Co-habiting couple’s maximum addition 6,400 6,400
1 Personal Allowance will be reduced by £1 for every £2 of income over £100,000 (over £200,000 for married
couple/civil partners).
1

Income tax rates 2026/2027 2025/2026
Individuals % %
Resident
Single: Lower rate on first £6,500 10 10
Married couple/civil partners - jointly assessed:
10 10
Lower rate on first £13,000
Higher rate on balance 21 21
Non-resident:
Non-resident rate on all income 21 21
Companies
Banking business 10 10
Land & property in the Isle of Man 20 20
Retail business - profits above £500,000 10 10
Petroleum extraction 20 20
All other income 0 0
Other non-corporates 21 21
For Fiscal Years commencing on or after 1 January 2025, groups in scope of Pillar 2 Global Minimum
Tax (large multinational enterprise groups with annual revenue of Euro 750 million or more in the
Consolidated Financial Statements of the Ultimate Parent Entity) will be taxed at a minimum effective
tax rate of 15% on their Isle of Man profits as a result of the introduction of Domestic Top-up Tax,
which is a new tax separate from (and in addition to) income tax. Further information is available on
the Government Website.
2. TT HOMESTAY
From 6 April 2026 the “TT Homestay Allowance” will be increased from £2,350 to £2,500.
This amount will be deducted from an individual’s gross rental income received under the Homestay
Scheme during the TT period and therefore, subject to certain conditions, will not be liable to income
tax. Please see Practice Note 219/22 for further details.
3. NURSING EXPENSES
The maximum amount of nursing expenses for which tax relief can be claimed in the tax year
commencing 6 April 2026 is increased from £12,500 to £15,000. Relief is granted on the amount
claimed at the lower tax rate of 10%.
2

4. NATIONAL INSURANCE CONTRIBUTIONS
The following rates and thresholds will apply for the 2026/2027 tax year commencing on 6 April 2026:-
Item (per week unless stated otherwise) 2026/2027 2025/2026
Lower Earnings Limit (LEL) £129 £125
Upper Accrual Point (UAP) N/A N/A
Upper Earnings Limit (UEL) £1,082 £1,032
Primary Threshold £176 £168
Secondary Threshold £176 £168
Prescribed annual equivalent of primary threshold £9,152 £8,736
Prescribed annual equivalent of secondary threshold £9,152 £8,736
Class 1 employees’ primary rate of NI (between primary 11% 11%
threshold and UEL)
Class 1 employees’ additional rate of NI (above the UEL) 1% 1%
Class 1 employers’ rate of NI (on all earnings above 12.8% 12.8%
secondary threshold)
Class 2 rate self-employed £6.75 £6.45
Class 2 small earnings exception level (annual) £9,152 £8,736
Class 2 rate for volunteer development workers £6.45 £6.25
Class 2 rate for share fishermen £8.30 £7.90
Class 3 voluntary contributions £20.65 £19.70
Class 4 lower profits limit (annual) £9,152 £8,736
Class 4 upper profits limit (annual) £56,264 £53,664
Class 4 rate between the lower & upper limits 8% 8%
Class 4 rate above the upper limit 1% 1%
5. INCREASE IN THE STATE PENSION AGE
From 6th April 2026 the state pension age will start to increase in increments from 66 years of age to
67 years of age for those born between 6th April 1960 and the 5th March 1961. Individuals who are
born between those dates will have a state pension age between 66 years and 1 month and 66 years
and 11 months and employees will continue to be liable to pay National Insurance up to their state
pension age. Employers are reminded that they remain liable to pay National Insurance after the
employee reaches their state pension age. Further information is available on the Division’s website.
3

Assessor of Income Tax
This Practice Note is intended only as a general guide and must be read in conjunction with the appropriate
legislation. It does not have any binding force and does not affect a person’s right of appeal on points concerning
their own liability to income tax.
Comments and suggestions for improvements of issued Practice Notes and suggestions for future Practice Notes
are always welcome.
4
